FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from 21 recruitment sites across the U.S.; ABCD 4.0 data release cited.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional and longitudinal analysis using multivariable linear mixed models on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere. All results use composite 24-hour movement behavior categories (e.g., Sleep Only, Screen Time Only, Sleep & Screen Time, All). Step 2: The screen time composite is derived from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey covering six activities: internet browsing, texting, video chatting, social media use, single player video games, and multiplayer video games. This composite bundles phone/SM items (texting, video chatting, social media) with non-phone/non-SM items (internet browsing, single player games, multiplayer games on unspecified platform). No per-modality breakout is reported in main results, figures, or supplementary tables. Composite fails Step 2.

notes: Paper examines adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines (sleep + screen time + physical activity) and cognitive, psychosocial, BMI, and brain morphometric outcomes. The screen time component is a bundled composite including gaming and internet browsing alongside social media and texting; no separate phone/SM breakout in any results table or figure.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; multi-center prospective cohort enrolling 11,875 children aged 9-10 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites; Year 2 and Year 3 data used.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional and longitudinal analysis using multivariable logistic regression on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only exposure variable is cyberbullying victimization (lifetime history and past-12-month history), measured by the question about being cyberbullied online, in texts, or on social media. No phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on social media, named platform use, texting frequency, etc.) appears anywhere in the paper with its own numeric estimate. Per binding clarification, cyberbullying alone fails IC2. Step 2 not reached. All numeric estimates (OR=2.17, 2.31, 2.22, 2.34, etc.) are for cyberbullying as predictor of substance initiation; no qualifying phone/SM variable is present.

notes: Cyberbullying victimization is the sole exposure. Binding clarification states cyberbullying alone does not qualify for IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient. No such variable exists in this paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 5,027 adolescents at 21 sites; ABCD Study Year 2 pre-pandemic data and COVID-19 rapid-response surveys (2020-2021) used; 3.0 release and COVID releases cited.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal and cross-sectional analysis using linear mixed-effects models (LMM) and generalized mixed-effects models (GLMM) on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Health Psychology; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds qualifying estimates. The paper separates screen time into two distinct variables: 'time spent on social media platforms' and 'single player video game time.' Social media use is an IV with its own numeric estimates across multiple sleep outcomes: time in bed (chi2=114.56, p<.01; simple slopesPre-pandemic=-0.24 through March=-0.12 all p<.01); bedtime (chi2=456.52, p<.01; simple slopesPre-pandemic=0.19 through March=0.09 all p<.01); sleep onset latency (chi2=33.74, p<.01); number of awakenings (chi2=6.94, p=.008). Sleep outcomes (TIB, bedtime, sleep onset latency, awakenings) are individual-trait-level health variables. Step 1 MET; stop.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported for social media use -> sleep outcomes: chi-square statistics, simple slopes (e.g., slopePre-pandemic=-0.24, p<.01 for social media ~ TIB), and confidence intervals throughout Tables S2-S7 and main results section.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential LMM/GLMM analyses with numeric results for the SM-sleep relationship reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is IV (predictor) in every analysis, predicting sleep outcomes. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis; that condition is not met here.
notes: Paper examines pre-pandemic and pandemic-period associations between social media use (and video gaming) as predictors and sleep outcomes (TIB, bedtime, sleep onset latency, awakenings) as outcomes. Social media time is clearly phone/SM-specific and serves as IV paired with individual-trait sleep outcomes. INCLUDE.
